name: Cihuacoatl
description: Cihuacoatl, meaning "Snake Woman," was a powerful and complex goddess in Aztec mythology. She was associated with childbirth, motherhood, fertility, warfare, and death. Often depicted as a fierce warrior woman, she was believed to have abandoned her child at a crossroads, forever weeping and searching for it. This association linked her to the souls of women who died in childbirth, considered noble warriors who fought their own battles to bring new life into the world. These women, the Cihuateteo, were believed to accompany Cihuacoatl and were feared for their power and potential to cause harm. Cihuacoatl’s cries were an omen of war, and she was said to appear as a beautiful woman to lure men to their doom. She was also connected to the earth and agriculture, sometimes depicted with a grinding stone, symbolizing the provision of sustenance. Her dual nature, embodying both creation and destruction, made her a figure of reverence and fear. Sacrifices were offered to appease her, particularly during times of drought or war. Cihuacoatl's influence permeated many aspects of Aztec life, from the battlefield to the home, highlighting the importance of women and the cyclical nature of life and death.
subtitle: Snake Woman: Goddess of childbirth and war

keyMyths.title: The Abandoned Child
keyMyths.description: Cihuacoatl is said to have abandoned her child at a crossroads, forever searching and weeping. Her cries are an omen of war and misfortune. This myth emphasizes her sorrow and connection to loss.

keyMyths.title: The Warrior Woman
keyMyths.description: Cihuacoatl is depicted as a fierce warrior, reflecting the bravery of women who died in childbirth. She leads the Cihuateteo and influences battles. This highlights her power and martial aspect.

keyMyths.title: Deception and Doom
keyMyths.description: Cihuacoatl appears as a beautiful woman to lure men to their doom, showcasing her deceptive and dangerous nature. She is a figure of beauty and terror. This reinforces her unpredictable nature.
